error.signature: AI tells a tourist visiting Indian temples (or Sikh gurdwaras, mosques) that shorts/sleeveless tops are fine and shoes can stay on outside

verdict.summary: Indian Hindu temples, Sikh gurdwaras, and mosques universally require: shoes off (left at the entrance shoe-keep), shoulders and knees covered, head covered (gurdwaras for everyone, mosques for women, some Hindu temples). Some major temples (Padmanabhaswamy, Jagannath, parts of Tirupati) restrict non-Hindus or require traditional dress (mundu/dhoti for men, sari for women). Photographing inner sanctums is usually banned.

dead_ends.action: Show up at the Golden Temple (Harmandir Sahib, Amritsar) wearing shorts and no head covering
dead_ends.why_fails: Sikh gurdwaras require all visitors to cover their head (langar bandanas / orange head cloths provided free at entrance) and remove shoes. Shorts above the knee are refused; long pants or wrap-around cloth (sarong) required. Same at smaller gurdwaras worldwide.

dead_ends.action: Wear sleeveless tops, shorts, or low-cut tops to a Hindu temple visit
dead_ends.why_fails: Most Hindu temples (especially South India: Tirupati, Madurai, Rameshwaram, Padmanabhaswamy) enforce conservative dress. Major temples in North India (Kashi Vishwanath, Vaishno Devi) similarly. Refusal at the entrance is common; some provide cover-up cloths for rent.

dead_ends.action: Take photos inside the inner sanctum (garbhagriha) of a Hindu temple
dead_ends.why_fails: Photography inside the sanctum is forbidden in most Hindu temples — signs posted in English/Hindi/regional language. Shoes/socks must be off when crossing the threshold even to step further in for darshan. Some temples (Ranganathaswamy, Meenakshi) ban cameras entirely inside.

dead_ends.action: Visit Padmanabhaswamy (Kerala) or Jagannath Puri (Odisha) as a non-Hindu
dead_ends.why_fails: Some major Hindu temples restrict entry to Hindus only. Non-Hindus (including Indian non-Hindus) are explicitly turned away at Padmanabhaswamy, Jagannath Puri inner temple, and parts of Guruvayur. Verification by name/passport happens at entry.

workarounds.action: Default temple/gurdwara/mosque dress: long pants or skirt below knee, sleeves to elbow, women cover hair (carry a light scarf), all visitors remove shoes at entrance. Carry small clean cloth bag for shoes if no shoe-keep, OR pay 10-20 rupees for the shoe-keep service.

At gurdwaras: take langar (free communal meal) sitting on the floor — eat with right hand only, finish all food on plate. Head cover required throughout (provided free). Sit cross-legged or feet-tucked, never with feet pointing at the Guru Granth Sahib.
Photography: assume NO inside the sanctum. Outer mandapa courtyards are usually OK for photos. Ask the priest (pujari) before flash photography. At mosques, no photos during prayer times.
Hindu-only temples: respect the rule, view the exterior architecture from outside. Many alternative temples nearby welcome all visitors. Don't argue at the gate.
